an3.61:1.1: "There are these three grounds of the sectarians, *bhikkhus*, which, when examined, pressed, and cross-questioned by the wise, ultimately come to rest on non-doing.
an3.61:1.3: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:1.4: 'Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is due to what was done in the past.'
an3.61:1.5: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:1.6: 'Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is due to creation by a supreme being.'
an3.61:1.7: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:1.8: 'Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is without cause or condition.'
an3.61:10.1: 'These six elements' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:10.3: With reference to what was it said?
an3.61:10.5: the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the wind element, the space element, the consciousness [*viññāṇa*] element.
an3.61:10.6: 'These six elements' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:10.7: So what was said was said with reference to this.
an3.61:11.1: 'These six dimensions [*āyatana*] of contact' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:11.3: With reference to what was it said?
an3.61:11.4: There are these six dimensions [*āyatana*] of contact, *bhikkhus*:
an3.61:11.5: the eye as a dimension [*āyatana*] of contact, the ear as a dimension [*āyatana*] of contact, the nose as a dimension [*āyatana*] of contact, the tongue as a dimension [*āyatana*] of contact, the body as a dimension [*āyatana*] of contact, the mind as a dimension [*āyatana*] of contact.
an3.61:11.6: 'These six dimensions [*āyatana*] of contact' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:11.7: So what was said was said with reference to this.
an3.61:12.1: 'These eighteen mental explorations' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:12.10: 'These eighteen mental explorations' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:12.11: So what was said was said with reference to this.
an3.61:12.3: With reference to what was it said?
an3.61:12.4: Having seen a form with the eye, one explores a form that can be a basis for joy, one explores a form that can be a basis for grief, one explores a form that can be a basis for equanimity [*upekkhā*];
an3.61:12.5: having heard a sound with the ear …
an3.61:12.6: having smelled a scent with the nose …
an3.61:12.7: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
an3.61:12.8: having touched a tangible with the body …
an3.61:12.9: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, one explores a *dhamma* that can be a basis for joy, one explores a *dhamma* that can be a basis for grief, one explores a *dhamma* that can be a basis for equanimity [*upekkhā*].
an3.61:13.1: 'These four noble truths' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:13.3: With reference to what was it said?
an3.61:13.4: Dependent upon the six elements, *bhikkhus*, there is descent into the womb;
an3.61:13.5: with descent there is *nāmarūpa*; with *nāmarūpa* as condition, the *saḷāyatana*; with the *saḷāyatana* as condition, contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
an3.61:13.6: For one who experiences *vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, I designate: 'this is *dukkha*,' 'this is the origin of *dukkha*,' 'this is the cessation of *dukkha*,' 'this is the path leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.'
an3.61:14.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the noble truth of *dukkha*?
an3.61:14.2: Birth is *dukkha*, aging is *dukkha*, (…) death is *dukkha*, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair are *dukkha*, association with what is disliked is *dukkha*, separation from what is liked is *dukkha*, not getting what one wants is *dukkha*. In brief, the five aggregates subject to clinging [*upādāna*] are *dukkha*.
an3.61:14.3: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the noble truth of *dukkha*.
an3.61:15.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the noble truth of the origin of *dukkha*?
an3.61:15.12: with birth as condition, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise.
an3.61:15.13: Such is the origin of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
an3.61:15.14: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the noble truth of the origin of *dukkha*.
an3.61:16.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the noble truth of the cessation of *dukkha*?
an3.61:16.10: with the cessation of clinging [*upādāna*], the cessation of *bhava*;
an3.61:16.11: with the cessation of *bhava*, the cessation of birth;
an3.61:16.12: with the cessation of birth, aging-and-death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair cease.
an3.61:16.13: Such is the cessation of this whole mass of *dukkha*.
an3.61:16.14: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the noble truth of the cessation of *dukkha*.
an3.61:16.2: With the remainderless fading away and cessation of blindness [*avijjā*], the cessation of *saṅkhāras*;
an3.61:16.3: with the cessation of *saṅkhāras*, the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*];
an3.61:16.4: with the cessation of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], the cessation of *nāmarūpa*;
an3.61:16.5: with the cessation of *nāmarūpa*, the cessation of the *saḷāyatana*;
an3.61:16.6: with the cessation of the *saḷāyatana*, the cessation of contact;
an3.61:16.7: with the cessation of contact, the cessation of *vedanā*;
an3.61:16.8: with the cessation of *vedanā*, the cessation of *taṇhā*;
an3.61:16.9: with the cessation of *taṇhā*, the cessation of clinging [*upādāna*];
an3.61:17.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of *dukkha*?
an3.61:17.2: It is this noble eightfold path, namely:
an3.61:17.3: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
an3.61:17.4: This, *bhikkhus*, is called the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.
an3.61:17.5: 'These four noble truths' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:17.6: So what was said was said with reference to this."
an3.61:2.1: There, *bhikkhus*, those ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:2.2: 'Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is due to what was done in the past' — I approach them and say:
an3.61:2.3: 'Is it true, venerable ones, that you hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:2.4: "Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is due to what was done in the past"?'
an3.61:2.5: And when questioned by me in this way, they affirm: 'Yes.'
an3.61:2.7: 'Then, venerable ones, those who take life do so because of what was done in the past; those who take what is not given do so because of what was done in the past; those who engage in sexual misconduct do so because of what was done in the past; those who speak falsely do so because of what was done in the past; those who speak divisively do so because of what was done in the past; those who speak harshly do so because of what was done in the past; those who speak idly do so because of what was done in the past; those who are covetous are so because of what was done in the past; those with ill will in mind are so because of what was done in the past; those with wrong view are so because of what was done in the past.'
an3.61:3.1: But, *bhikkhus*, for those who fall back on what was done in the past as their essence, there is no desire or effort, no sense of 'this should be done' or 'this should not be done.'
an3.61:3.2: When what should and should not be done cannot be found to be truly and reliably established, those who dwell with *sati* muddled and unguarded have no legitimate personal claim to the title of ascetic.
an3.61:3.3: This, *bhikkhus*, is my first legitimate refutation of those ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine.
an3.61:4.1: There, *bhikkhus*, those ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:4.2: 'Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is due to creation by a supreme being' — I approach them and say:
an3.61:4.3: 'Is it true, venerable ones, that you hold this view and doctrine —
an3.61:4.4: that whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is due to creation by a supreme being?'
an3.61:4.5: And when questioned by me in this way, they affirm: 'Yes.'
an3.61:4.7: 'Then, venerable ones, those who take life do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who take what is not given do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who engage in sexual misconduct do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who speak falsely do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who speak divisively do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who speak harshly do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who speak idly do so because of creation by a supreme being; those who are covetous are so because of creation by a supreme being; those with ill will in mind are so because of creation by a supreme being; those with wrong view are so because of creation by a supreme being.'
an3.61:5.1: But, *bhikkhus*, for those who fall back on creation by a supreme being as their essence, there is no desire or effort, no sense of 'this should be done' or 'this should not be done.'
an3.61:5.2: When what should and should not be done cannot be found to be truly and reliably established, those who dwell with *sati* muddled and unguarded have no legitimate personal claim to the title of ascetic.
an3.61:5.3: This, *bhikkhus*, is my second legitimate refutation of those ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine.
an3.61:6.1: There, *bhikkhus*, those ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine:
an3.61:6.2: 'Whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is without cause or condition' — I approach them and say:
an3.61:6.3: 'Is it true, venerable ones, that you hold this view and doctrine —
an3.61:6.4: that whatever a person experiences — *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*sukha*-nor-*dukkha* — all that is without cause or condition?'
an3.61:6.5: And when questioned by me in this way, they affirm: 'Yes.'
an3.61:6.7: 'Then, venerable ones, those who take life do so without cause or condition … those with wrong view are so without cause or condition.'
an3.61:7.1: But, *bhikkhus*, for those who fall back on there being no cause or condition as their essence, there is no desire or effort, no sense of 'this should be done' or 'this should not be done.'
an3.61:7.2: When what should and should not be done cannot be found to be truly and reliably established, those who dwell with *sati* muddled and unguarded have no legitimate personal claim to the title of ascetic.
an3.61:7.3: This, *bhikkhus*, is my third legitimate refutation of those ascetics and brahmins who hold this view and doctrine.
an3.61:8.1: These, *bhikkhus*, are the three grounds of the sectarians which, when examined, pressed, and cross-questioned by the wise, ultimately come to rest on non-doing.
an3.61:9.1: And there is this *dhamma*, *bhikkhus*, taught by me, which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:9.2: And what is this *dhamma*, *bhikkhus*, taught by me, which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins?
an3.61:9.3: 'These six elements' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:9.4: 'These six dimensions [*āyatana*] of contact' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:9.5: 'These eighteen mental explorations' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
an3.61:9.6: 'These four noble truths' — this, *bhikkhus*, is a *dhamma* taught by me which is irrefutable, undefiled, beyond reproach, not rejected by wise ascetics and brahmins.
